era_context: Caliph Uthman's caliphate (644–656 CE) is in its early-to-mid period, consolidating Islamic authority. Byzantine Emperor Constans II is engaged in military campaigns and religious disputes. Tang Dynasty under Emperor Gaozong (r. 649–683 CE) is about to begin his reign; his father Emperor Taizong is still ruling (r. 626–649 CE). South Indian Pallava and Chalukya dynasties remain in competition.

events.title: Arab conquest of Alexandria (Egypt)
events.description: Arab forces under the command of Amr ibn al-As completed the conquest of Alexandria, the major Byzantine city in Egypt. This conquest was the culmination of the Arab conquest of Egypt, which began in 639 CE. Alexandria's fall marked the end of Byzantine control over Egypt and the establishment of Islamic authority. The conquest was strategically and economically significant, as Alexandria was a major Mediterranean port and center of trade. The conquest involved both military siege and negotiation, with the Byzantine garrison eventually withdrawing or being defeated.

sources.name: Byzantine administrative records and military accounts

events.certainty_note: The Arab conquest of Alexandria is attested in both Islamic and Byzantine sources, but the exact dating is uncertain. Some sources place it in 646 CE, others in 645 or 647 CE. The broader pattern of Arab conquest of Egypt is confirmed.
cross_references: Part of broader Arab conquest of Egypt (639–646 CE)

events.title: Byzantine religious council and theological disputes
events.description: Emperor Constans II was engaged in theological disputes regarding Monothelitism (the doctrine that Christ had one will) and other religious controversies. These disputes were significant in Byzantine religious and political life, as they involved questions of imperial authority, ecclesiastical organization, and doctrinal orthodoxy. The theological disputes were part of the broader Byzantine struggle to maintain religious unity in the face of doctrinal disagreements. These disputes would eventually lead to the Council of Constantinople (680–681 CE) and the condemnation of Monothelitism.

sources.name: Byzantine ecclesiastical records and theological treatises

events.certainty_note: Byzantine theological disputes during Constans II's reign are well-attested, but specific events in 646 CE are difficult to date precisely.

events.title: Tang Dynasty under Emperor Taizong (final years)
events.description: Emperor Taizong of Tang (r. 626–649 CE) was in the final years of his reign, having established Tang as a major power in East Asia and Central Asia. Taizong's reign was marked by military expansion, administrative consolidation, and cultural development. The emperor was preparing for the succession of his son, the future Emperor Gaozong. Taizong's reign is considered one of the high points of Tang power and influence. The period saw the consolidation of Tang control over Central Asia and the establishment of the Silk Road trade network.
key_figures: Crown Prince Li Zhi (future Emperor Gaozong)

sources.name: Tang administrative records and literary works

events.certainty_note: Emperor Taizong's reign is well-documented in Chinese historiography. The final years of his reign (646–649 CE) are clearly attested.

events.title: Pallava-Chalukya conflict in South India
events.description: The Pallava and Chalukya empires continued their military competition for regional dominance in South India. The period following Pulakeshin II's death (642 CE) saw the Pallavas gaining some advantage under Narasimhavarman I. The conflict involved military campaigns, territorial disputes, and dynastic rivalries. These conflicts shaped the political geography of South India and influenced the development of regional kingdoms. The period is marked by sophisticated military organization and the patronage of temples and arts by both dynasties.

sources.name: Pallava and Chalukya inscriptions and temple records
Later Tamil and Sanskrit literary sources

events.certainty_note: The Pallava-Chalukya conflict is attested in inscriptions and later literary sources, but specific events in 646 CE are difficult to date precisely.
cross_references: Part of longer Pallava-Chalukya conflict cycle (6th–8th centuries CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Arab conquest of Alexandria to 646 CE is disputed; some sources place it in 645 or 647 CE. The extent of Byzantine resistance and the nature of the conquest (military siege vs. negotiated surrender) are debated. The specific theological disputes in 646 CE are difficult to isolate from the broader pattern of Byzantine religious controversy. The Pallava-Chalukya conflict attribution to specific rulers and years relies heavily on inscriptional evidence, which can be ambiguous regarding exact dating and causation.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 646 CE is similar to previous years: Islamic sources are abundant but late; Byzantine sources are fragmentary; Chinese sources are detailed and reliable; South Indian sources are epigraphic and require careful interpretation. The record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events. Economic and social developments are underrepresented. The historical record is shaped by the survival of certain types of sources and the loss of others. Egyptian sources are particularly sparse, relying heavily on later Islamic and Byzantine accounts. The conquest of Alexandria is a major event, but the details of the conquest and its immediate aftermath are not fully documented in surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: Completion of Arab conquest of Egypt, which began in 639 CE
Byzantine theological disputes eventually led to the Council of Constantinople
Emperor Taizong's reign ended with the succession of Emperor Gaozong
